news_summary: Today's news highlights a growing recognition that personal challenges and unconventional paths, such as low publication rates, do not equate to failure, promoting a more compassionate and inclusive understanding of success in human rights and academia.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière une prise de conscience croissante selon laquelle les difficultés personnelles et les parcours atypiques, tels que le faible nombre de publications, ne sont pas synonymes d'échec, favorisant ainsi une conception plus bienveillante et inclusive de la réussite dans les domaines des droits de l'homme et du monde universitaire.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy ponen de relieve la creciente conciencia de que los retos personales y los itinerarios poco convencionales, como los bajos índices de publicación, no equivalen a un fracaso, lo que fomenta una visión más comprensiva e inclusiva del éxito en el ámbito de los derechos humanos y el mundo académico.

articles.title: Reckoning with my ‘ghost years’: why a low publication rate doesn’t always reflect failure
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-025-04062-9I have a five-year gap in my publication record. Last year, I published seven academic articles as either the first or corresponding author. Here’s why one level of output isn’t better than any other.
articles.content:
Israel Sebastian / Getty

‘Publish or perish’ is a common mantra in academia. By that metric, I definitely did not perish in 2025.

Last year, I published seven scholarly articles as either the first or corresponding author. As an ex-postdoc who became a research scientist studying astrobiology at Carnegie Science in Washington DC towards the end of last year, I’ve been writing academic articles for more than a decade. I have never before experienced such a bounty of publications. Last year’s publications represent more than 40% of my first- and corresponding-author papers. By almost any measure, it was a wildly successful year. But I’m not here to brag. Rather, I want to point out that just because last year was prolific, it does not mean that the preceding years in which my research output was lower were not also successful.

Invisible progress

I did not produce a single lead-author paper between my final publication as a graduate student in 2017 and the first paper I published as a postdoc in 2022. Having such a huge gap in your publication record usually spells disaster for an early-career researcher. We are judged mainly on our output of scientific publications. The larger the volume of papers and the higher their impact, the better. But any peer-reviewed product is infinitely better than nothing, especially when your career is young and your CV is short. Going several years without a first-author paper is seen as a red flag in the academic job market.

Gender gap in research publishing is improving — slowly

So what was I doing from 2017 to 2022? During those ‘ghost years’, it might have seemed to the outside world that I had disappeared from science, but I wasn’t slacking off in the laboratory. I was discovering myself as a researcher, diving head first into my field of astrobiology, learning methods and developing research concepts — activities that set me on the path to becoming the scientist I am today. Although I contributed as a co-author on a few manuscripts, the projects I led had frustrating false starts; some ended up going nowhere. But when I think about those five paperless years, I don’t feel disappointment and I don’t see failure. Instead, I’m proud of how much I grew. The seven papers I published last year would not exist without those fallow years.

Personal satisfaction alone, however, is not a recipe for survival in academia. If it weren’t for the good graces of more-senior academics looking out for me, my research career certainly would have perished as a result of my lack of publications. Without mentors who supported me, believed in me and took chances on me, I would have left science by now.

Pressure to publish

I know many people who have left (or are considering leaving) academia because they’ve been made to feel that their publication rate was not high enough — and that they, therefore, were not enough. Although it is only one of myriad factors at play, concern over publication rate and staying productive is a contributor to anxiety in people who work in academia1. When success is distilled into simple metrics, it’s easy to compare and compete. A cut-throat environment doesn’t only lead to poor research, it can drive people to leave science altogether. In fact, nearly half of all scientists depart academia less than a decade after publishing their first paper2.

How can we resist the most tragic effects of the publish-or-perish mentality? Here are four suggestions.

How ChatGPT and other AI tools could disrupt scientific publishing

First, we must recognize that research takes time — often much longer than a single year. Each of the seven papers I published in 2025 represents several years of hard work that happened to culminate this year. In fact, one of those projects began in 2020; another’s seeds were planted at a workshop held at Carnegie Science in 2022. Unfortunately, many postdoctoral research stints are shorter than the time it takes to see ambitious scientific projects from inception to publication, and research has shown that uncertainty from short-term contracts increases career vulnerability3. We have to find ways to give slow productivity the chance to pay off, and we have to give ourselves and each other the grace to let years go by when science simply takes that long to develop.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the challenges and stigma around publication gaps in academia, emphasizing the importance of recognizing diverse paths to success and the need for supportive mentorship. It addresses systemic issues affecting many researchers, promoting a more inclusive and understanding academic culture, which has broad societal implications for equity and mental health in science careers.
articles.personality_title: Why quiet years in science can lead to big discoveries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In science, researchers often face pressure to publish many papers quickly. This is called the 'publish or perish' rule. But sometimes, it takes years of work before important results appear in papers. 

**What happened** – A scientist shared her story of having a five-year gap with no first-author papers from 2017 to 2022. Despite this, she kept learning and working on ideas in astrobiology. In 2025, she published seven papers as the lead author, which was a big success. She explains that those quiet years were important for her growth and future achievements.

**Impact** – This story shows that success in science is not only about how many papers are published each year. It highlights how long research can take and how gaps in publishing don’t always mean failure. Having mentors who support researchers during slow times is critical. This understanding can help reduce stress and encourage more people to stay in science.

**What’s next step** – The scientist suggests that academic culture should change to value slow and steady progress. Giving researchers time and support will allow important discoveries to develop. This may also help improve fairness and mental health in science careers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Taking time to grow and explore ideas quietly can lead to major scientific achievements later on.
articles.personality_title_fr: Pourquoi les années calmes en science peuvent mener à de grandes découvertes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En science, les chercheurs sont souvent sous pression pour publier rapidement beaucoup d’articles. C’est ce qu’on appelle la règle « publier ou périr ». Mais parfois, il faut des années de travail avant que des résultats importants apparaissent dans des articles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une scientifique a raconté son histoire : elle a eu une pause de cinq ans sans article en tant qu’auteur principal entre 2017 et 2022. Pourtant, elle a continué à apprendre et à travailler sur des idées en astrobiologie. En 2025, elle a publié sept articles en tant qu’auteure principale, un grand succès. Elle explique que ces années calmes ont été importantes pour sa progression.

**Impact** – Cette histoire montre que le succès en science ne se mesure pas seulement au nombre d’articles publiés chaque année. Elle souligne que la recherche prend du temps et que des pauses dans les publications ne signifient pas toujours un échec. Avoir des mentors pour soutenir les chercheurs durant ces périodes est essentiel. Cette compréhension peut aider à réduire le stress et encourager plus de personnes à rester en science.

**Prochaine étape** – La scientifique suggère que la culture académique devrait évoluer pour valoriser les progrès lents et réguliers. Donner du temps et du soutien aux chercheurs permettra aux découvertes importantes de se développer. Cela pourrait aussi améliorer l’équité et la santé mentale dans les carrières scientifiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Prendre le temps de grandir et d’explorer des idées en silence peut conduire à de grandes réussites scientifiques plus tard.
articles.personality_title_es: Por qué los años tranquilos en la ciencia pueden llevar a grandes descubrimientos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la ciencia, los investigadores suelen sentir presión para publicar muchos artículos rápidamente. Esto se llama la regla de 'publicar o desaparecer'. Pero a veces, la investigación lleva años antes de que aparezcan resultados importantes en los artículos.

**Qué pasó** – Una científica compartió su historia de tener un período de cinco años sin artículos como autora principal entre 2017 y 2022. A pesar de eso, siguió aprendiendo y trabajando en ideas de astrobiología. En 2025, publicó siete artículos como autora principal, un gran logro. Explica que esos años tranquilos fueron importantes para su crecimiento y éxitos futuros.

**Impacto** – Esta historia muestra que el éxito en la ciencia no depende solo de cuántos artículos se publiquen cada año. Destaca que la investigación toma tiempo y que las pausas en la publicación no siempre significan fracaso. Contar con mentores que apoyen a los investigadores en esos momentos es fundamental. Esta visión puede ayudar a reducir el estrés y animar a más personas a quedarse en la ciencia.

**Próximo paso** – La científica sugiere que la cultura académica debe cambiar para valorar el progreso lento y constante. Dar tiempo y apoyo a los investigadores permitirá que se desarrollen descubrimientos importantes. Esto también podría mejorar la equidad y la salud mental en las carreras científicas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Tomarse el tiempo para crecer y explorar ideas en silencio puede llevar a grandes logros científicos más adelante.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly lit study scene featuring a young sapling growing steadily inside a clear glass terrarium filled with layered soil and tiny scientific tools scattered around—symbolizing slow but persistent growth and unseen effort—set against a warm, muted background of open notebooks, scattered research papers, and a glowing vintage desk lamp casting gentle light, evoking patience, nurturing, and the quiet progression of a researcher's journey.
